source: Admitting a 68-year-old woman with a history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, her EF was 35 percent on the last echo, also has hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and chronic kidney disease stage 3. She comes in with three days of progressive shortness of breath, orthopnea — sleeping on three pillows now instead of one — and lower extremity swelling. She admits she ran out of her furosemide about a week ago. On exam she's got an elevated JVP, bibasilar crackles, and 2+ pitting edema to the knees. BNP is markedly elevated at 1850, creatinine is 1.6 up from her baseline of 1.2, chest x-ray shows pulmonary vascular congestion. This is an acute heart failure exacerbation, most likely from diuretic non-adherence. I'm going to restart IV furosemide, diurese her, monitor daily weights and strict ins and outs, daily BMP given the kidney function. Continue her carvedilol and lisinopril for now, hold metformin while inpatient. Her hypertension and diabetes are stable. Cardiology is on board for the heart failure management.
reference:
HPI: 68F with HFrEF (EF 35%), HTN, T2DM, and CKD stage 3 presents with 3 days of progressive dyspnea, orthopnea (3 pillows, up from 1), and lower-extremity edema. Reports running out of furosemide ~1 week ago.

Exam: Elevated JVP, bibasilar crackles, 2+ pitting edema to the knees.
Data: BNP 1850 (markedly elevated). Creatinine 1.6 (baseline 1.2). CXR: pulmonary vascular congestion.

Assessment/Plan:
# Acute HFrEF exacerbation, likely diuretic non-adherence — restart IV furosemide, diurese, daily weights, strict I/O, daily BMP given renal function. Cardiology consulted.
# Acute kidney injury on CKD3 — likely cardiorenal; monitor with diuresis.
# Hypertension — continue carvedilol and lisinopril.
# Type 2 diabetes — hold metformin while inpatient.
Admit to hospital medicine.
